original_abstract: Despite the rapid proliferation of carbon dot (CD) research, the fundamental mechanisms governing their synthesis remain a subject of intense debate. This study addresses this gap by employing a one-step hydrothermal method to produce highly efficient, blue-fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) from 4-aminoantipyrine. By systematically varying the reaction time from 3 to 21 hours, we investigated the structural evolution and formation pathway of the NCDs using a multi-spectroscopic approach, including FT-IR, 1H-NMR, 13C-NMR, and UV-visible spectroscopy. Our findings reveal that as hydrothermal treatment progresses, the aromatic character of the precursor diminishes due to enhanced noncovalent $\pi$–$\pi$ stacking and the hydrolysis of the pyrazole ring. This process leads to the formation of an amorphous carbon core (particle size ~5 nm) progressively encapsulated by polar aliphatic functional groups, such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amine moieties. Notably, this structural transformation correlates with a steady increase in photoluminescent quantum yield. Characterized by HR-TEM and XRD, these NCDs demonstrate excellent water solubility and a graphitic lattice spacing of 0.26 nm. This work provides a comprehensive mechanical insight into CD formation and offers a scalable, cost-effective route for synthesizing high-quality NCDs for diverse optoelectronic and biological applications.
humanized_abstract: The growth behavior of carbon dots (CDs) is a concern at the molecular level, which is still controversial, hampering the fast development of CDs research. In the present work, highly fluorescent blue nitrogen doped carbon dots (NCDs) are synthesized in situ by means of a one-step hydrothermal strategy based on 4-aminoantipyrine at different growth times ranging from 3 to 21 h. Through FT-IR, 1H NMR, 13C NMR, and UV-vis spectroscopy, the evolution behavior and forming mechanism are explored in detail. The results demonstrated that the increase of hydrothermal time endows precursor more aromatic and noncovalent π–π interaction, meanwhile breaking the pyrazole ring at the resulting NCDs are amorphous in structure with a particle size of about 5 nm in diameter with an increasing carbon core being coated progressively with polar aliphatic groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl and amine. Consequently, the fluorescence quantum yield increases with the increasing growth time. Structure and morphology are characterized by HR-TEM and XRD, showing good aqueous stability, and exhibiting a lattice spacing of 0.26 nm. In summary, the whole growing mechanism was explored precisely, and an economic method was applied to synthesize highly fluorescent blue NCDs efficiently which can have great potential in the fields of material science and biomedicine.
